_comment: Bugfix bake-off narrative deck. Scene types (title / cards / narrative) match stories/slidey-edit/baked/deck.json + stories/slidey-edit/schemas/deck.json so it bakes to static HTML via `slidey bundle` (see deck/README.md). Numbers are the real bug9 results from results/cells/*.json; pending grid cells are marked 'pending (quota/queue)', not invented.

scenes.title: The Bugfix Bake-off
scenes.subtitle: Is structure worth more than a bigger model?
scenes.narration: We hold the bugs fixed and vary two things — the structure of the workflow, and the model behind it — then grade every run against a hidden oracle.

scenes.body: Does the kitsoki pipeline make fixes better, and how does that trade against the model you point at it? We vary structure — the seven-room bugfix pipeline versus a single multi-stage prompt — and model — GLM-5.2, Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.5 — over the same real bugs. If a cheaper model with structure matches a frontier model without it, structure is the lever and model is just the cost knob.
scenes.narration: The question those leave open: does structure make fixes better, and how does that trade against the model? We vary the workflow structure and the model over the same real bugs.

scenes.title: The experiment: a 2×4 grid over 3 bugs
scenes.narration: For each real bug we run a two-by-four grid: two treatments by four candidates, all from the fix's hermetic parent commit, graded against the fix's own regression test as a hidden oracle — with adjudication for oracles that are coupled to canonical wording.

scenes.body: Each cell runs in its own disposable worktree from the fix's parent commit, pre-flighted RED. The hidden oracle stays out of the candidate's tree until scoring. Wording-coupled oracles false-fail correct fixes, so a judge adjudicates on behaviour — keeping the raw oracle status. Cost is one basis: kitsoki's native per-call cost, and claude -p priced from recorded usage on a corrected rate table that reproduces native cost to within half a percent.
scenes.narration: Each cell is hermetic and pre-flighted red. The oracle is hidden until scoring, behavioural adjudication rescues correct fixes done differently, and cost is reported on one consistent basis with tokens as the primary axis.

scenes.title: bug9 — single-prompt + guidance
scenes.caption: The naive baseline: one staged prompt, oracle-gated guidance turns counted. Opus solved it cold; Sonnet needed one behavioural nudge for a third of the cost.

scenes.title: bug9 — kitsoki pipeline
scenes.caption: The structured pipeline: pricier, but it wrote its own regression test and parked safely at the gate. Sonnet's failure here — a 65-test-breaking fix — is what forced the prompt hardening (commit d210ea67).

scenes.title: Grid coverage — 24 cells (3 bugs × 4 models × 2)

cells: opus+sonnet done · GLM blocked · GPT pending

scenes.caption: Methodology validated end-to-end on bug9; the rest is queued. The harness is resumable — each cell re-runs independently and the committed summary.json regenerates this deck offline.

scenes.body: On bug9, the kitsoki pipeline on Opus cost $7.73 — about 1.9× the same model under a single prompt at $4.00, and roughly 3.5× the wall time. The pipeline's overhead is real. Structure buys thoroughness, not a smaller bill, when the single prompt happens to land a correct fix unaided.
scenes.narration: Structure is not automatically cheaper. The pipeline on Opus cost nearly twice the same model under a single prompt. The overhead is real.

scenes.body: The kitsoki cell chose a different valid fix — a per-session worktree path, the ticket's own proposed resolution — wrote its own regression test, ran a refine loop, and parked at a red gate instead of declaring victory. The single-prompt cells solved the behaviour but added no regression test and left the suite red. The pipeline never shipped an unverified fix.
scenes.narration: Only the pipeline wrote its own regression test, ran a refine loop, and parked at a red gate. The single-prompt runs solved the behaviour but left the suite red and added no test.

scenes.lede: A cheaper model matched the frontier one.
scenes.body: Sonnet under a single prompt reached the same behavioural solve as Opus for $3.02 with one feedback turn. The model axis is at least as consequential as the structure axis on this bug — which is exactly the trade the study is built to measure across the full grid.
scenes.narration: Sonnet matched Opus on the same bug for three dollars with one feedback turn. The model axis matters as much as structure.

scenes.title: The dogfood payoff: the pipeline caught a bad fix
scenes.narration: Building the kitsoki treatment was a dogfood marathon. The headline finding: the hardened pipeline catches fixes a naive single prompt ships unverified.

cards.label: F1 — caught & hardened
cards.sub: blind implementer shipped a 65-test-breaking fix → self-verify before submit (d210ea67)

cards.label: F3 — uncleaned worktree
cards.sub: park leaves branch + no resume hint

scenes.body: Three of forty cells are scored — all on bug9. GLM-5.2 cells are blocked on a synthetic.new quota wall. bug12, bug14, and the sonnet/glm/gpt kitsoki cells are queued. This is a validated method plus a first worked example, not a finished sweep — the empty cells are pending, not zero.
scenes.narration: Three of forty cells are scored. The GLM column is blocked on quota and the rest are queued. This is a validated method and a first result, not a finished sweep.

scenes.lede: Structure is the lever. Model is the cost knob.
scenes.body: The framework is reusable: drop new bugs into bakeoff.yaml, point it at new candidates, regrade against hermetic baselines and hidden oracles, regenerate the report and deck offline with zero re-spend. The run distilled into two general skills — matrix-task-comparison and dogfood-marathon — with task-bakeoff and dogfood-marathon stories to follow.
scenes.narration: The framework is reusable — add bugs, add candidates, regrade offline with no re-spend. It distilled into two skills and two forthcoming stories, ready for the full grid.
